section.title: Israel's Only Savior
section.sectionRationale: Continuing the Book of Comfort, this chapter presses the polemic against idols into its most personal form yet: the LORD who formed Israel and calls it by name declares Himself the only Savior there has ever been or ever will be, promising a new exodus through the waters and the desert, even as He honestly names Israel's failure to call on Him at all.
section.thesis: The LORD who formed and redeemed Israel declares Himself its one and only Savior, before whom no god was formed and after whom none will come, and promises a new way through the wilderness even for a people who, by their own admission, grew weary of Him and did not call upon His name.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: do not fear, for I have redeemed you; the promised gathering of exiles from every direction; the courtroom declaration that the LORD alone is God and Savior; a further word of coming rescue from Babylon; the announcement of a new thing, a way in the wilderness; Israel's failure to call on the LORD in worship; and the closing promise that the LORD blots out transgressions for His own sake.

units.label: Do not fear, I have redeemed you

verses.text: Now this is what the LORD says, He who {{bara|created}} you, O Jacob, and He who formed you, O Israel: “Do not fear, for I have redeemed you; I have called you by your name; you are Mine!

When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and when you go through the rivers, they will not overwhelm you. When you walk through the fire, you will not be scorched; the flames will not set you ablaze.
For I am the LORD your God, the Holy One of Israel, your Savior; I give Egypt for your ransom, Cush and Seba in your place.
Because you are precious and honored in My sight, and because I love you, I will give men in exchange for you and nations in place of your life.

units.label: I will bring your offspring from every direction

verses.text: Do not be afraid, for I am with you; I will bring your offspring from the east and gather you from the west.
I will say to the north, ‘Give them up!’ and to the south, ‘Do not hold them back!’ Bring My sons from afar, and My daughters from the ends of the earth,
everyone called by My name and created for My glory, whom I have indeed formed and made.”
Bring out a people who have eyes but are blind, and who have ears but are deaf.

All the nations gather together and the peoples assemble. Who among them can declare this, and proclaim to us the former things? Let them present their witnesses to vindicate them, so that others may hear and say, “It is true.”

“You are My witnesses,” declares the LORD, “and My servant whom I have chosen, so that you may consider and believe Me and understand that I am He. Before Me no god was formed, and after Me none will come.
I, yes I, am the LORD, and there is no Savior but Me.
I alone decreed and saved and proclaimed, I, and not some foreign god among you. So you are My witnesses,” declares the LORD, “that I am God.
Even from eternity I am He, and none can deliver out of My hand. When I act, who can reverse it?”
Thus says the LORD your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: “For your sake, I will send to Babylon and bring them all as fugitives, even the Chaldeans, in the ships in which they rejoice.
I am the LORD, your Holy One, the Creator of Israel, and your King.”

units.label: Behold, I am about to do something new

verses.text: Thus says the LORD, who makes a way in the sea and a path through the surging waters,
who brings out the chariots and horses, the armies and warriors together, to lie down, never to rise again; to be extinguished, snuffed out like a wick:
“Do not call to mind the former things; pay no attention to the things of old.
Behold, I am about to do something new; even now it is coming. Do you not see it? Indeed, I will make a way in the wilderness and streams in the desert.
The beasts of the field will honor Me, the jackals and the ostriches, because I provide water in the wilderness and rivers in the desert, to give drink to My chosen people.
The people I formed for Myself will declare My praise.

units.label: You have not called on Me

verses.text: But you have not called on Me, O Jacob, because you have grown weary of Me, O Israel.
You have not brought Me sheep for burnt offerings, nor honored Me with your sacrifices. I have not burdened you with offerings, nor wearied you with frankincense.
You have not bought Me sweet cane with your silver, nor satisfied Me with the fat of your sacrifices. But you have burdened Me with your sins; you have wearied Me with your iniquities.
I, yes I, am He who {{machah|blots out}} your transgressions for My own sake and remembers your sins no more.
Remind Me, let us argue the matter together. State your case, so that you may be vindicated.
Your first father sinned, and your spokesmen rebelled against Me.
So I will disgrace the princes of your sanctuary, and I will devote Jacob to destruction and Israel to reproach.”

bara.gloss: To create, a verb Scripture reserves exclusively for God's own creative act, used first of the heavens and the earth in Genesis 1:1. Here it is applied personally to Israel, the same God who created the cosmos also created this particular people for Himself, grounding their identity and security in His creative, not merely their own historical, origin.

machah.gloss: To blot out, wipe away, erase completely, as one wipes a slate clean or a name from a record. The LORD declares He blots out Israel's transgressions for His own sake, not because they earned it, and the same word pictures total, irreversible removal, not partial or temporary forgiveness, a promise the New Testament shows fulfilled completely in Christ.

basic.context: This chapter moves from tender reassurance, do not fear, I have redeemed you, to a courtroom scene where the LORD alone stands as God and Savior, to the electrifying announcement of a new exodus through the wilderness, and finally to an honest and painful diagnosis: Israel had grown weary of God and stopped calling on Him, yet He still promises to blot out their sin for His own sake.
basic.hermeneutics: The immediate promise addresses exiles who will be gathered from Babylon and beyond, a real historical return under Cyrus. But its language of passing through waters and fire, and making a way in the wilderness, deliberately echoes the exodus from Egypt, presenting the return from exile as a new exodus, a pattern of redemption that reaches its fullest sense in Christ's own greater deliverance from sin and death.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel is the people the LORD Himself {{bara}}, created, and formed, not a nation that made itself, and this same LORD promises to {{machah}}, blot out, its transgressions completely, for His own sake rather than because of anything Israel has done to deserve it. Both words underline that Israel's whole existence, from creation to forgiveness, rests entirely on God's initiative.
basic.typology: The new way through the wilderness, streams in the desert, and a path through the sea, verses 16 to 20, deliberately echoes the exodus from Egypt and anticipates the greater new exodus Christ accomplishes, His own decisive act of deliverance that Luke 9:31 actually calls His 'exodus,' accomplished at Jerusalem. The declaration that there is no Savior but the LORD, verse 11, anticipates the New Testament's insistence that salvation is found in no one but Jesus, Acts 4:12.
basic.christ: The LORD's declaration, 'I, yes I, am the LORD, and there is no Savior but Me,' verse 11, is fulfilled in Jesus, of whom Peter says, 'there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved,' Acts 4:12. The promise that God 'blots out your transgressions for My own sake and remembers your sins no more,' verse 25, is the same complete forgiveness secured at the cross, since only God can forgive sins, Mark 2:7, and Jesus does exactly that.
basic.god: God is Israel's Creator, Redeemer, and only Savior, before whom no rival god was ever formed and after whom none will come. He is honest about His people's weariness and failure to call on Him, yet He forgives not because they earned it but for His own name's sake, blotting out transgressions completely and remembering them no more.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 4:12 declares salvation is found in no name but Jesus, echoing the LORD's exclusive claim in verse 11. Mark 2:7-10 shows Jesus forgiving sins, an authority the Pharisees rightly recognize belongs to God alone. Luke 9:31 calls Christ's coming death His 'exodus,' fulfilling the new exodus imagery of this chapter. 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 treats the first exodus as a type pointing forward to Christ.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's movement from redemption, through a courtroom declaring the LORD the only Savior, to a new exodus, and finally to grace despite Israel's weariness, traces the whole Bible's pattern of God's initiative in saving a people who cannot save themselves. It anticipates Christ, the only Savior, whose own exodus at the cross accomplishes the final and complete blotting out of sin this chapter promises.

context.literary: Structured in seven movements alternating between tender personal reassurance and courtroom declaration, moving from redemption and gathering, through the LORD's exclusive claim to be Savior, to the new exodus, and closing with an honest reckoning of Israel's failure met by undeserved grace.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles in Babylon, promising both a literal gathering of scattered Israelites from every direction and, in verse 14, the specific downfall of Babylon itself, later accomplished through Cyrus of Persia, named explicitly in chapters 44 to 45.
context.canonical: Develops the polemic against idols and the incomparability of the LORD begun in chapter 41, and its new exodus language anticipates both the return from exile and, more fully, the New Testament's presentation of Christ's death and resurrection as the true and final exodus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure a fearful, exiled people that the LORD who created and redeemed them is the only Savior there is, capable of a new exodus through the wilderness, and to confront them honestly with their failure to call upon Him, while still promising forgiveness grounded in God's own character rather than their merit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's specific historical promises to exiled Israel and diagnoses their specific failure in worship; it is not a template guaranteeing identical circumstances for every reader. Its abiding principle is that salvation belongs to God alone, that forgiveness is granted for His own name's sake, and that even weary, unfaithful worship does not exhaust His mercy.

debates.question: Is the 'new thing' and 'way in the wilderness,' verses 18 to 19, primarily about the historical return from Babylonian exile, or does it point beyond that to something greater?

views.label: Primarily the historical return from exile
views.case: The immediate context describes the literal gathering of exiles, verses 5 to 7, and Babylon's fall, verse 14, so the new way in the wilderness most directly describes the physical journey home from captivity.

views.label: A pattern pointing to the ultimate new exodus in Christ
views.case: The deliberate echoes of the exodus from Egypt, a path through the sea, water in the desert, suggest this return functions as a type of a greater deliverance still to come, one the New Testament identifies as Christ's own 'exodus' accomplished at the cross, Luke 9:31.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together: a real historical return from exile that also functions, by design, as a pattern anticipating the fuller and final new exodus God accomplishes in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's whole identity rests on being {{bara}}, created, and formed by the LORD Himself, not self-made, and its whole hope rests on His promise to {{machah}}, blot out, its transgressions completely, for His own sake rather than any merit of its own. Both terms place the initiative for Israel's existence and forgiveness entirely with God.

typology.type: A new way through the wilderness, echoing the exodus from Egypt
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own decisive act of deliverance, explicitly called His 'exodus' at Jerusalem, Luke 9:31.

typology.type: The LORD as the only Savior, before whom no other god exists
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, in whose name alone salvation is found, Acts 4:12.

study.christ: The LORD's exclusive claim, 'I, yes I, am the LORD, and there is no Savior but Me,' verse 11, finds its New Testament fulfillment in Jesus, of whom Peter declares, 'there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved,' Acts 4:12. The promise that God 'blots out your transgressions for My own sake and remembers your sins no more,' verse 25, is precisely the forgiveness only God can grant, Mark 2:7, and precisely what Jesus accomplishes fully and finally at the cross.
study.god: God is Israel's Creator, Redeemer, and only Savior, uniquely and exclusively so, before whom no rival god was ever formed and after whom none will come. He names His people's weariness and failure honestly rather than overlooking it, yet He still chooses to forgive, blotting out transgressions completely for the sake of His own name rather than any merit they could offer.

crossReferences.note: Declares salvation found in no name but Jesus, the direct fulfillment of the LORD's exclusive claim in verse 11.
Jesus forgives sins, an authority the religious leaders rightly recognize belongs to God alone, fulfilling verse 25.
Calls Christ's coming death at Jerusalem His 'exodus,' fulfilling the new exodus imagery of this chapter.
Treats the original exodus through the sea as a type pointing forward to the salvation found in Christ.

study.oneStory: This chapter's movement from redemption, through the LORD's exclusive claim to be the only Savior, to a promised new exodus, and finally to grace despite Israel's weariness, traces the whole Bible's pattern of a God who initiates salvation for a people who cannot save themselves. It anticipates Christ, the only Savior, whose own exodus at the cross accomplishes the final and complete blotting out of sin this chapter promises.

quiz.q: What promise does the LORD give in verses 1 to 2 regarding waters and fire?

options.text: When Israel passes through waters or fire, the LORD will be with them and they will not be overwhelmed or scorched.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 state this promise of presence and protection directly.

options.text: Israel will never face any hardship or trial again.
options.note: The promise is God's presence through trials, not the total absence of trials.

options.text: Only the wealthy among Israel will be protected.
options.note: The promise is given to the whole people, called by name, not a wealthy subset.

options.text: Israel must earn this protection through additional sacrifices.
options.note: The promise flows from being redeemed and called by name, not from additional required sacrifices.

quiz.q: What exclusive claim does the LORD make in verse 11?

options.text: There is no Savior but Me.
options.note: Verse 11 states this exclusive claim directly, echoed in Acts 4:12 regarding Christ.

options.text: Other nations have equally valid saviors.
options.note: The opposite; the LORD claims to be the only Savior, with no rivals.

options.text: Salvation is available through many different gods.
options.note: The verse insists on exclusivity, not a plurality of valid saviors.

options.text: Salvation will come only through human effort.
options.note: The claim is that the LORD alone saves, not that salvation depends on human effort.

quiz.q: What does the LORD announce He is about to do in verses 18 to 19?

options.text: A new thing, making a way in the wilderness and streams in the desert.
options.note: Verse 19 announces exactly this new thing, echoing the exodus pattern.

options.text: A return to exactly the former things, with nothing new.
options.note: The opposite; the LORD explicitly says not to dwell on the former things, since He is doing something new.

options.text: The permanent destruction of Israel with no restoration.
options.note: The announcement is one of hope and provision, not destruction.

options.text: A command to remain in exile permanently.
options.note: The announcement describes a way of deliverance and provision, not permanent exile.

quiz.q: What honest diagnosis does the LORD give of Israel in verses 22 to 24?

options.text: They grew weary of Him, did not call on Him, and burdened Him with their sins rather than honoring Him with sacrifices.
options.note: These verses state this diagnosis directly and honestly.

options.text: Israel had perfectly fulfilled every requirement of worship.
options.note: The opposite; the passage describes failure and neglect in worship, not perfect fulfillment.

options.text: Israel over-sacrificed to the point of exhausting the priests.
options.note: The text says the opposite: Israel did not bring sacrifices or honor God with offerings.

options.text: The failure described belongs only to a few individuals, not the nation.
options.note: The diagnosis is addressed to Jacob and Israel collectively, describing a national pattern, not isolated individuals.

quiz.q: On what basis does the LORD blot out Israel's transgressions in verse 25?

options.text: For His own sake, not because Israel earned or deserved it.
options.note: Verse 25 states this directly: 'for My own sake,' grounding forgiveness in God's character, not merit.

options.text: Because Israel offered sufficient sacrifices to earn forgiveness.
options.note: The preceding verses explicitly say Israel had not honored God with sacrifices; forgiveness is not earned here.

options.text: Because Israel's ancestors had lived faultlessly.
options.note: The following verses actually recall the sin of Israel's 'first father' and spokesmen, not faultless ancestry.

options.text: Because Babylon requested it on Israel's behalf.
options.note: The forgiveness is entirely between the LORD and Israel, grounded in God's own character, not a third party's request.

questions: The LORD promises His presence through waters and fire, not the absence of trials. What waters or fire are you currently walking through, and how does knowing God is with you in it change your posture?
The LORD declares there is no Savior but Him, a claim the New Testament applies fully to Jesus. Where are you tempted to look for salvation, security, or rescue outside of Christ?
God announces a new thing and asks, 'do you not see it?' What might God be doing that is new in your life right now that you have not yet noticed or acknowledged?
Israel's failure was not dramatic rebellion but simply growing weary of God and no longer calling on Him. Has your own relationship with God grown into quiet neglect rather than active dependence?
God blots out transgressions for His own sake, not because they are earned. How does resting in grace rather than performance change the way you approach your own failures this week?

translationNotes.note: Egypt, Cush, and Seba given 'in your place' most likely reflects the historical pattern of Cyrus receiving these regions in exchange for releasing the Jewish exiles, though the text frames it as God's own sovereign exchange for His people's sake.
The Hebrew idiom behind 'even now it is coming' emphasizes the nearness and certainty of this promised new deliverance, not a distant or merely hoped-for future event.
Your first father most likely refers to Jacob, or possibly Adam, as the archetypal ancestor whose sin set the pattern the nation's spokesmen, its later leaders, continued to repeat.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 43:1-28. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
